title: Phishing attacks
description: Check your understanding of phishing attacks in this set of free practice questions designed for AP Computer Science Principles students.
topic_path: Going deeper with recognizing and avoiding online scams

assessment_items.question_content:
Dax is a customer of the payment company BeanMo and typically logs on to "beanmo.com".

He receives an email that claims to be from BeanMo and contains a link to a webpage. 

[[☃ image 2]]

He clicks the link but then starts to wonder if the email may be a phishing attack, as it looks different from their usual emails.

Once the webpage loads, he checks the URL in the browser to see if it's really a BeanMo URL.

**Which of these URLs is most likely a legitimate BeanMo domain?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Screenshot of an email with sender "BeanMo <beanmo@mail.com>" and body text "Hi Dax, Your transaction history for January 2020 is now available." followed by a button "View history on BeanMo.com"

choices.content: www.beanmo.ac.at
account.beanmo.com
beanmo-account.com
beanmo.account.com

assessment_items.question_content:
Sigourney has an account on the social media website InstaTag and typically logs on to the domain name "instatag.com".

She receives an email that claims to be from InstaTag and contains a link to a webpage. 

[[☃ image 1]]

She clicks the link but then realizes that the email may be a phishing attack, as she hasn't had any recent trouble logging into InstaTag.

Once the webpage loads, she checks the URL in the browser to see if it's really an InstaTag URL.

**Which of these URLs is most likely owned by InstaTag?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

options.alt: Screenshot of an email from "Instatag <instatag@mail.com>" with the text "Hi SigSpider, Sorry to hear you're having trouble logging into InstaTag. We can help you get straight back into your account." It contains a button that says "Log in as SigSpider" and a link "You can also reset your InstaTag password."

choices.content: www.instatag.us
user.instatag.com
instatag-user.com
instatag.user.com

assessment_items.question_content:
Sacha uses a payment app called CircleCash and typically logs on to his merchant account at "circle.com".

He receives an email that claims to be from CircleCash and contains a link to a webpage. 

[[☃ image 1]]

He clicks the link but then realizes that the email may be a phishing attack, as he didn't request a password reset recently.

Once the webpage loads, he checks the URL in the browser to see if it's a legitimate CircleCash URL.

**Which of these URLs is most likely owned by CircleCash?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

options.alt: Screenshot of an email from "Circle <circlecash@mail.com>"  with the heading "Reset your CircleCash password", the text "We received a request to change your CircleCash account password. To reset your password, press the Reset Password button below within 24 hours.", and a button labeled "Reset Password".

choices.content: www.circle.io
io.circle.com
circle-io.com
circle.io.com

assessment_items.question_content:
Karlee receives this email that claims to be from Instagram, the social media site:

[[☃ image 2]]

**Which aspect of the email is _least_ indicative of a phishing attack?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
Screenshot of email that says:

Subject: !! New login to your Instagram account !!
From: I-nstagram <instasupport@i-n-s-t-a-gram.com>
Body:

We noticed a new login from a device you don't usually use:
Chrome Mobile, in Oslo, Norway, on Thu Feb 28 2:55PM

If this was you, you can disregard this email.
If this wasn't you, Click here to protect your account.
© Instagram. Facebook Inc. 1601 Willow Road, Menlo Park, CA 94025

choices.content: The subject line uses multiple exclamation marks.
The sending email address is from a non-Instagram domain.
The text of the email suggests clicking on a link.
The email footer includes the company's mailing address.
The text of the email contains alarming information about their account security.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nisa receives this security alert about her Google account:

[[☃ image 3]]

She suspects that it's a phishing email, so she decides not to click the buttons at the bottom.

**Which aspect of the email is _least_ indicative of a phishing attack?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
Screenshot of email that says:

Subject: ⚠️We've Detected Unusual Activity on Your Account ! ⚠️
From: Security Alert <99x45w45@guuglemundo.com>
Body:
Connecting to a new device
A user has just signed in to your Google Account from a new device. We are sending you this email to verify that it is you.
Location : india mumbai

Two buttons at bottom say "Yes me !" and "Not me !"

choices.content: The subject line uses warning signs and an exclamation mark.
The sending email address is not from a google.com domain.
The email recommends clicking buttons to take further action.
The email includes a company logo.
The email uses non-standard spacing and capitalization.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tatsuki receives an urgent email from his bank:

[[☃ image 1]]

He thinks it might be a phishing scam, so he decides to ignore it.

**Which aspect of the email is _least_ indicative of a phishing attack?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
Screenshot of email that says:

Subject: Urgent: Barclay’s Bank Tax Documents
From: Bank Support <barclaysbank@freemail.co>
Body:
Dear Bank Customer,
The federal government requires us to have up-to-date account information for all accounts.
You must download, fill out, and send back the attached form.
If we do not have form by next week, your account will be frozen!

The bottom shows an attached document named "form.html"

choices.content: The subject line and body use alarming language.
The sending email address is not from an official company domain.
The email recommends downloading an attachment.
The email references the federal government.
The email asks the recipient to reply with account information.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mason receives an email from a law firm called "Baker & McKenzie". The email states that they are scheduled to appear in court and includes a link to view the court notice.

Mason is suspicious that this may be a phishing attack, since this is the first they've heard about a court appearance.

**What would be the safest next step?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They can look in a phone directory for a number to the listed court and call the court to see if they have record of the notice.
They can click the link to see if it links to a suspicious-looking website.
They can reply to the email and ask for further proof of legitimacy.
They can forward to a friend to ask for their opinion about the suspicious nature of the email.

assessment_items.question_content:
Evelyn receives an email that claims to be from the IRS, the United States Internal Revenue Service. The email states that their tax refund is ready and includes an attachment labeled "taxrefund.doc".

Evelyn is eager for their refund but worried the email is a phishing scam. 

**What is the safest next step?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Evelyn can find the official IRS website by searching the Web and contact IRS through a listed email address to inquire about the email.
Evelyn can download the attachment, send it through their antivirus software, and confirm that it has no malware inside it. Then they can safely open it.
Evelyn can search the Web to see if the ".doc" file type can ever contain malware. If it is always safe, then they can open it.
Evelyn can open the attachment from their mobile phone instead of from their personal computer, since the mobile phone is more malware-proof.

assessment_items.question_content:
A teacher receives an email to his school address. The email claims to be from their school's learning management system and asks him to verify his account credentials. 

He follows a link in the email to a website with a username and password box. The website doesn't look quite right, so he suspects it might be a phishing scam.

**What would be the safest next step?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Follow his bookmarked link to the LMS website, and email their official support address to see if the email is real.
Type in his real username but use a fake password, so that he can see what's on the next screen.
Check if the website is using HTTPS, and if so, he can safely enter his credentials.
Call the help phone number listed on the bottom of the website and inquire about the legitimacy of the email.

assessment_items.question_content:
Agatha received an email from her bank that asked for verification of her account details. She clicked the link in the email and entered her username and password into a form.

[[☃ image 2]]

At that point, she realized the email was a phishing scam, and she had just revealed her password to the cyber criminals behind the scam.

**What are the effects of revealing her bank account password to cyber criminals?**

👁️**Note that there are 2 answers to this question.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Screenshot of webpage with heading of "Verify account" and a form with 2 input fields. First field is labeled "Username" and second field is labeled "Password". Button says "Submit".

choices.content: They can use the password to login to the real bank website.
They can try the password on other websites where she has a login.
They can use the password to access the entire database of the banking website.
They can now more easily figure out other people's passwords for the banking site.
They can log into her computer and look at the files on her hard drive.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jaime received an email from his favorite movie streaming service. The email stated that all customers were being asked to reset their passwords (due to a security breach) and linked to a webpage with a password reset form. The webpage asks him to confirm his old password and then enter a new password.

[[☃ image 1]]

At that point, Jaime wonders if the email and webpage are part of a phishing scam. He searches the Web and finds other reports of the scam, so he closes the webpage and marks the email as spam.

**What could have happened if he had filled out the form?**

👁️**Note that there are 2 answers to this question.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Screenshot of webpage that has a form with 2 input fields. First field is labeled "Old password" and second field is labeled "New password". Button says "Reset password".

choices.content: The attackers could use Jaime's "old password" to login to the actual movie streaming website.
The attackers could try using the "old password" and "new password" to login to Jaime's accounts on other sites.
The attackers could login to the administrative control panel of the movie streaming website and steal the data of all their users.
The attackers could reset the password for all the users of the movie streaming website.
The attackers could add malware to the actual website's home page, so that all users get infected by it.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sahed works for admissions at a university. One day, she receives an email about a new admissions tool that she needs to start using ASAP.  The email links to a webpage with a registration form.

[[☃ image 3]]

She decides to re-use her password from the old administration tool, and signs up with her email and that password. 

Unfortunately, she then receives a message from her manager that the email is a phishing scam targeting everyone in their department, and that she should ignore it.

**What are the effects of revealing that password to the cyber criminals?**

👁️**Note that there are 2 answers to this question.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Screenshot of webpage with heading of "Create account" and a form with 2 input fields. First field is labeled "Email" and second field is labeled "Password". Button says "Register".

choices.content: The cyber criminals can try that password on the actual admissions tool and successfully gain access.
The cyber criminals can infect the servers of the actual admissions tool with malware.
The cyber criminals could use the password to login as any user of the actual admissions tool.
The cyber criminals can disable her ability to access her university computer and ask her to pay a ransom.
The cyber criminals can sell her email and password combination to other attackers.
